entry.title: Re: Integration Between SAP Datasphere and SAP Analytics Cloud - Blog 1
entry.content:
Hi @sachin_prabhudesai5,

Thank you very much for your comment/query.

Yes, you can use Datasphere to act as a data source to integrate with SAC for importing actuals into a planning model. You will need to set up an OData connection with an OAuth client which will allow for this. This setup enables secure and efficient data transfer between the two platforms. I have discussed this in blog 4 of this blog series which should be posted in the next few days. 

For now, please see below link to assist you:

  • Integrate with SAP Analytics Cloud for Planning

Hope this helps!

entry.title: Where can I find materials to develop the skills to implement data provisioning in SAP BW/4HANA?
entry.content:
We  recommend to access our Implementing Data Provisioning in SAP BW/4HANA learning journey

Overview
This learning journey will enable you to develop the skills to implement data provisioning in SAP BW/4HANA.

Learning objectives
After completing this learning journey, consultants will be able to:

  • Identify data provisioning approaches in SAP BW/4HANA
  • Identify source systems and their use cases
  • Load data from SAP source systems using ODP_SAP DataSources
  • Explain delta loads using ODP_SAP DataSources
  • Provide data from SAP S/4HANA using ABAP CDS Views
  • Explain delta loads using ABAP CDS Views
  •  

Please ask a question related to the digital learning Journey in the Q&A area.

Our SAP Learning Experts will get back to you as soon as possible! We are here to support you.

We appreciate your feedback and we will make sure to continue sharing interesting topics.

entry.title: Re: Digital Assistant availability and cross application needs
entry.content:
1) As it's meant to improve UX, forcing people to write in a language they are not confident in defeats the purpose. Also, AI is not great at dealing with language errors - especially "false friends". But other languages are coming very soon, as far as I understand
2) That would be an important feature. I'd expect it to by on short term roadmap - maybe SAP colleagues can comment?
3) Maybe not a huge issue for early days, but again, I'd expect this to change, as users expect a one-stop-shop. 

At the current stage, which I would describe as experimentation anyway (I guess nobody in their right mind is relying on a convAI bot as the only or main communication channel of an importing topic, today), imo customers can live with it, but I really expect these gaps to be closed no later than 2025.

entry.title: UI5 custom control input in Planning Calendar don't get focus
entry.content:
Hi everyone,

For a proof of concept, we decided to use the PlanningCalendar control with a custom content aggregation for the CalendarAppointment.

So I created a simple custom control:

 

 

renderer = {
    apiVersion: 2,
    render: (rm: RenderManager, control: Appointment) => {
      const text = new Text({
        text: `${control.getText()}${control.getDecoration()}`,
        visible: !control.getEdit(),
      })
      

      if (control.getUnderline()) {
        text.addStyleClass('underline')
      }

      rm.openStart('div', control)
      rm.class('appointment-style')
      rm.openEnd()

      rm.renderControl(text)

      rm.openStart('input')
      rm.attr('id', `${control.getId()}-input`)
      rm.attr('type', 'text')
      rm.attr('value', control.getText())
      rm.attr('style', `display: ${control.getEdit() ? 'block' : 'none'}`)
      rm.class('appointment-input')
      rm.openEnd()
      rm.close('input')
      
      rm.close('div')
    },

 

 

The idea of the custom control is very simple, it's a div containing a UI5 Text control and an HTML Input. When the user click on the control, the Text control becomes invisible and the input becomes visible so the user can simply modify the content of the tile.

I set up the event assignment in the `onAfterRendering` function:

 

 

onAfterRendering(oEvent: JQuery.Event): void | undefined {
    const id = this.getId()

    this.$().on('click', () => {
      if (!this.getEdit()) {
        this.setEdit(true)
      }

      const input = jQuery(`#${id}-input`)

      if (input) {
        input.on('focus', () => {
          console.log('Input focused')
        })

        input.on('blur', () => {
          console.log('Input blurred')
        })

        input.trigger('focus')
        console.log('Input focused') // Debugging line
      }
    })
  }

 

 

The issue is the following, if I click on the tile and the input is displayed, there is no way to select or modify the text, the focus doesn't work at all.

As you can see in the code, I tried to "force" the focus event and yes the console logs works for the focus or the blur, but it is still impossible to select or modify the value of the input.

My guess is that another HTML Element in the tree is overlapping my custom control and blocking the input modification.

The problem with this guess, is that IT IS possible to select the text when the Text control is visible and the input is invisible.

entry.title: Datasphere: Accessing target structure in Python Script
entry.content:
Hello,

I'm working on rebuilding a complex BW transformation that we have, in Datasphere. The transformation has an expert routine which I'm trying to recreate using Python script inside a Data Flow. In the ABAP expert routine, I have access to the target structure via 

 

RESULT_PACKAGE

 

and I can use 

 

ASSIGN COMPONENT x OF STRUCTURE RESULT_FIELDS

 

 to access a component of the structure if it exists. This is convenient as I can use this to ignore fields from the source which I'm not interested in.

The question is, is there a similar mechanism in Datasphere? Can I somehow access the target structure within the script? My assumption is that it's not possible as there is only one Dataframe called 'data' to begin with. With that said, if there is no other way, is my only option to hardcode the columns of the result DataFrame with all the fields that I expect and check for each column before assigning any values. Is there a more elegant way? 

Thanks in advance.

entry.title: Data Engineering is the Future - the Rise of the Data Engineer
entry.content:
You might have received an email from SAP Learning informing you that your C_HANAIMP_XX or C_HCMOD_XX certification has been retired, or you found this information on the SAP Certification Transformation | SAP Learning page.

Both channels provide the correct information. In this BLOG it will explain the why and the future for the SAP HANA certifications.

 

Why the change?

There are two main reasons for the retirement of C_HANAIMP and C_HCMOD.

  • SAP has transformed itself into a cloud company in recent years. This cloud vision is not reflected in the C_HANAIMP certification, because this certification is based on an on-premises product. The C_HCMOD would fit perfectly into the cloud vision, however, the accompanying learning material is only focusing on modelling, the data provisioning area isn’t touched.
  • SAP Learning's certification strategy has changed from product-based to role-based certifications. The C_HANAIMP_XX and C_HCMOD are product-based certification.

To meet the cloud and role-based strategy of SAP, I decided to break with the old, retiring C_HANAIMP and C_HCMOD, and move forward to something new. The new certification C_HAMOD - SAP Certified Associate - Data Engineer - SAP HANA is role-based and reflects the SAP cloud strategy. If you want to learn more about the role of a Data Engineer, have a look at the excellent blog Plumbing in the Data – the rise of the Data Engine... - SAP Community from my colleague Mark Green.

 

We made sure that all the shared modeling topics from the courses HA300 – SAP HANA Modeling, the SAP Learning Journey HC300 – SAP HANA Cloud Modeling were merged, and the new SAP Learning Journey HAHC55 – Provisioning Data to SAP HANA was added.

All the mentioned SAP Learning Journeys are available, for free, on learning.sap.com to everyone interested in the SAP HANA knowledge.

 

The C_HAMOD Topics

When you start studying for the C_HAMOD_2404 the following SAP Learning Journeys are important:

  • HC300 - Developing Data Models with SAP HANA Cloud
  • HAHC55 - Provisioning Data to SAP HANA

A Data Engineer - SAP HANA needs thorough knowledge of Modeling on SAP HANA. Data modeling in SAP HANA Cloud is very similar to data modeling on SAP HANA. This means that when you already have the HA300 knowledge, this would be sufficient and skimming through the HC300 would be a great refresher for the certification. If you have no prior data modeling knowledge in SAP HANA, you only need to study the learning journey HC300 - Developing Data Models with SAP HANA Cloud.

In today's world, as a Data Engineer, you also need to know how to provision data to SAP HANA. The knowledge on how to do this is available in the learning journey HAHC55 – Provisioning Data to SAP HANA.

Another change is version numbering. The certification C_HAMOD now has the YYMM version numbering. The C_HAMOD became available in April 2024, so the name of the current certification is thus C_HAMOD_2404. Next April, the C_HAMOD_2504 will be released.

 

Do I need to recertify myself?

The short answer: No

The long answer: The holders of a C_HANAIMP_15 to C_HANAIMP_18 certification will be automatically migrated to the Stay Certified program and will receive the new certification C_HAMOD - SAP Certified Associate - Database Administrator - SAP HANA. No further action needed.

The same is true for the holders of a C_HCMOD_XX certification, you will also receive the new certification C_HAMOD - SAP Certified Associate - Database Administrator - SAP HANA.

If you hold a C_HANAIMP_XX and a C_HCMOD_XX certification, then of course you only once get the C_HAMOD certification.

 

The Stay Certified program

On the 2nd of April 2024 SAP Learning launched the Stay Certified program. The new SAP Certification program makes it easier and faster for you to gain and maintain the latest skills and valid credentials on the key SAP technologies and solutions for your job.

In the Stay Certified program, you only need to pass the C_HAMOD certification once to become a certified database administrator. After passing the exam, you can renew the certification every year by passing the Stay Certified Assessment questions. The assessment questions are based on the Stay Certified material made available each year.

The Stay Certified program offers you the opportunity to keep your certification up to date by answering questions on new SAP HANA topics each year via the Stay Certified assessment.

The Stay Certified program is SAP Learning answer to the frequently expressed desire from the learners to offer delta courses and delta certifications.

Important links:

  • Homepage: SAP Learning
  • Learning Journey: HC300 - Developing Data Models with SAP HANA Cloud
  • Learning Journey: HAHC55 - Provisioning and Administering Databases in SAP HANA Cloud
  • Classroom training: HA300 - SAP HANA 2.0 SPS07 Modeling
  • SAP Certification Transformation on SAP Learning
  • SAP Learning Hub

Hay Bouten
SAP Learning - SAP HANA Portfolio Manager

entry.title: Clarification regarding accounting principle assignment / S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition
entry.content:
We are implementing the S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition system for a Client from the Automotive Industry and have a question about the ledger and accounting principle assignments. Currently, ledger setup and respective accounting principles is as following:

0L – Leading ledger, combining local GAAP principles for two company codes (Polish and Mexican). Both companies have the same YCOA chart of accounts.

2L – Non leading ledger assigned to one IFRS principle, for both company codes.

Therefore, we would like to ask for your recommendation regarding such setup and know if there are any possible future constraints.

Please note that both companies share the same fiscal year which corresponds with the standard calendar year and there is no plan to change it.

Moreover, a lot of transactional data was already posted due to ongoing tests.

Thank You! 
Best regards,
Michal Czubaszewski

entry.title: Function Calling LLMs for SAP: Structured Outputs and API calling
entry.content:
Introduction

Large Language Models (LLMs) are evolving from serving knowledge passively in chatbots to actively interacting with applications and services in JSON, YAML or other structured outputs. But in our world this is old as the world.

What is new is Agentic systems, co-pilots, plugins, function calling and tool use, all them steps towards this direction bringing innovative solutions to old the old of problem of remote function calling. Yes, RFC.

The logical next step in this evolution is toward autonomous LLM-powered microservices, services, and applications.

Yes, LLMs have been around for some years, but Function calling models is a relatively new concept. Function Calling was first "released" by OpenAI in mid-2023 to enable large language models (LLMs) to reliably interact with external tools and APIs. From the model. Even we have a fantastic blog by @yogananda  in SAP to use "OpenAI Function Calling" with SAP BTP. Start there, because I go some steps further.

How function calling works is developers describe custom functions to the models, which could then output JSON containing arguments to intelligently call those functions when needed, based on the user's input. This is called "in context learning", in other words, we tell the model what we want the model to know, we give knowlege, in context.

By doing this, we extend the capabilities of LLMs beyond just text-based reasoning. Function calling enables the models to access real-time information, interact with databases and APIs, extract structured data from text, solve multi-step problems, and more things. It's important for Enterprise, because we have multiple sources of information, multiple tools, that, if lucky, we can access through API. SAP as an example, how many APIs we have in BTP, and how many custom APIs we built on S/4HANA.

And I go one step further than limiting this to OpenAI, recently other companies like Anthropic, Google, and open-source projects have been releasing models with function calling abilities, but who has been trained (if any) on SAP APIs is absolutely fundamental. Other than that, everytime I hear SAP "collaboration" with other vendors on Generative AI models, such as their collaboration with Google Gemini, OpenAI, IBM, Aleph Alpha, Anthropic or Cohere, I wonder, but what is that collaboration about? are they training a model on SAP APIs? Why is this important? I will explain this in this blog.

---

If the goal is to build systems that talk to other systems, and not chatbots, then you want complete control over your prompts. Many libraries pre-write the prompts, making it easy to start but requiring significant work to undo these pre-written elements months into a project. When OpenAI function calling was introduced, the usage of JSON schema led to new ways of prompting and working with language models by using Pydantic for example which is a well used library.

OpenAI has trained these models to call functions, so they inherently know how to do it, unlike open-source models where you have to explain everything in the prompts or in the training.

 

Function calling, but we do it, not the model

Don't get confused, one could think that function calling in a model means that the model calls functions. And that would be a great feature for a model to have, but the model has no ability to call functions when you use function calling. Function calling is all about controlling the format of the output from the model. When you get the response in a format you expect, then you can reliably call a function. But the user, or the application is calling the function. The model is not.

This expansion of LLM capabilities is, if lucky, extensive with third-party service providers (e.g., Slack, Gmail, Dropbox, etc.), enabling seamless interactions between users and services through personalized LLM workflows. These third-party agents would allow users to communicate with and through them using customized LLM-powered workflows, and integrate a wide range of services.

Cool, but all that you just read, is LLM dependent

You must inform the model about the functions, and this information is model dependent. This is just how you let the model know about the schema the output should stick to. The chat completion message shows the output string is formatted to JSON, so the model must know the schema.

In OpenAI, this is the purpose of the function block.

But every model has its own requirements, if you are curious what are the requirements to "teach" an LLM how to call an API, check this

LLM requirements for API call

Llama 3 model requires this, forgive me for bringing code into the blog 🙏🏼

 

 

 

[
    {
        "type": "function",
        "function": {
            "name": "get_current_weather",
            "description": "This function gets the current weather in a given city",
            "parameters": {
                "type": "object",
                "properties": {
                    "city": {
                        "type": "string",
                        "description": "The city, e.g., San Francisco"
                    },
                    "format": {
                        "type": "string",
                        "enum": ["celsius", "fahrenheit"],
                        "description": "The temperature unit to use."
                    }
                },
                "required": ["city"]
            }
        }
    },
    {
        "type": "function",
        "function": {
            "name": "get_clothes",
            "description": "This function provides a suggestion of clothes to wear based on the current weather",
            "parameters": {
                "type": "object",
                "properties": {
                    "temperature": {
                        "type": "string",
                        "description": "The temperature, e.g., 15 C or 59 F"
                    },
                    "condition": {
                        "type": "string",
                        "description": "The weather condition, e.g., 'Cloudy', 'Sunny', 'Rainy'"
                    }
                },
                "required": ["temperature", "condition"]
            }
        }
    }    
]

 

 

 

functions / contains function files, each function needs its JSON file with a specific structure that describes a function and its sample prompts and responses.

LangChain Output Parser will help?

The LangChain output parser can be used to create structured output, and JSON can be the choice if desired.

The two main implementations of the LangChain output parser are:

Get format instructions: A method which returns a string containing instructions for how the output of a language model should be formatted.

Parse: A method which takes in a string (assumed to be the response from a language model) and parses it into some structure.

The problem is JSON is quite prone to errors in its formation, or it might not be formed at all, and in Enterprise, we must control the output, then we use Pydantic.

What is Pydantic doing in all this?

Getting JSON output from Llama 3 , by setting the model format to JSON and defining a JSON schema helps extract information in a structured format. Using a JSON output parser returns a dictionary.

The problem of this approach is that responses are then obtained using Ollama functions and Pydantic to define a class schema. We have control, but then this is not automatic, every schema needs to be identified. This is not enough.

And on top of Pydantic, we use Instructor and Marvin

Instructor elevates Pydantic to create a more intuitive and streamlined experience when working with language models. Instructor is limited to OpenAI Function Calling, but if this is NOT a problem for you, it allows developers to work with structured data. Despite its advantages, it still has complexities around JSON Schema. This is exactly the thing, the implementation of Instructor requires storing in Vector DB, Define Pydantic Model to specify the structure of the extracted data, pass the document or instruction, and Model to Instructor, and finally calling LLM Using Instructor, mostly OpenAI.

Marvin is more mature than Instructor and has more tools, probably is not so focus on its goal, specially if your objective is using OpenAI Function Calling, but its tools are simple and self-documenting, and help with complex challenges like entity extraction, classification, and generating synthetic data.

Let's forget frameworks then, I want models to be good at Function calling

All described above will required a significant amount of development, especially if we have large API datasets and very clearly defined APIs such as the ones we have in an SAP Workflow.

OpenAI without Function Calling

 

No API ⬆️

With API ⬇️

OpenAI with weather function calling 

Could be good to have some chances of rain in Barcelona 🥹

In February 2024, Fireworks AI released FireFunction V1, a model with GPT-4 level function calling accuracy, open-source models like Mistral-7B and Dolphin-2.7 also demonstrated function calling by early 2024. 

Will the tiny, small models save the day?

Keep in mind that large language models are leaky abstractions! You'll have to use an LLM with sufficient capacity to generate well-formed JSON, not every model will be useful. Take Gemma-2B as an example, take our API. Generate 1,000 "correct" API function call responses by dint of placing only your API calls in the pre-prompt, then prompting it.

To create a function call for comparing two bills of materials (BOMs) using the SAP S/4HANA On-Premise API, we need to refer to the specific API endpoints and methods provided in the documentation. Based on the provided sources, here is the JSON structure for the function call that can be used for OpenAI function calling in context learning for fine-tuning:

JSON Structure for Function Call API ⬇️

 

{
  "functionCalls": [
    {
      "question": "How do I compare two bills of materials (BOMs)?",
      "request": {
        "method": "POST",
        "endpoint": "/sap/opu/odata/sap/API_BILL_OF_MATERIAL_SRV/CompareBOMs",
        "headers": {
          "Content-Type": "application/json"
        },
        "body": {
          "BOMComparison": {
            "BOM1": {
              "BOMHeader": {
                "BOM": "BOM_ID_1",
                "Plant": "PLANT_1"
              },
              "BOMItems": [
                {
                  "ItemNumber": "0010",
                  "Material": "MATERIAL_1",
                  "Quantity": "10"
                },
                {
                  "ItemNumber": "0020",
                  "Material": "MATERIAL_2",
                  "Quantity": "20"
                }
              ]
            },
            "BOM2": {
              "BOMHeader": {
                "BOM": "BOM_ID_2",
                "Plant": "PLANT_2"
              },
              "BOMItems": [
                {
                  "ItemNumber": "0010",
                  "Material": "MATERIAL_3",
                  "Quantity": "15"
                },
                {
                  "ItemNumber": "0020",
                  "Material": "MATERIAL_4",
                  "Quantity": "25"
                }
              ]
            }
          }
        }
      },
      "response": {
        "status": 200,
        "body": {
          "ComparisonResult": {
            "Differences": [
              {
                "ItemNumber": "0010",
                "BOM1Material": "MATERIAL_1",
                "BOM2Material": "MATERIAL_3",
                "BOM1Quantity": "10",
                "BOM2Quantity": "15"
              },
              {
                "ItemNumber": "0020",
                "BOM1Material": "MATERIAL_2",
                "BOM2Material": "MATERIAL_4",
                "BOM1Quantity": "20",
                "BOM2Quantity": "25"
              }
            ]
          }
        }
      }
    }
  ]
}

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

This JSON structure is designed to be used for OpenAI function calling in context learning for fine-tuning, based on the API details provided in the SAP S/4HANA On-Premise documentation, it will not serve other models.

Note that they use "functional tokens" in training - they convert a function to a particular, previously unused tokenization, and refer to it that way. They claim this speeds up inference (I'm sure it does). They don't make any claims as to whether or not it changes their accuracy (I bet that it does). It definitely makes the system more fragile / harder to train for large and very large APIs.

Outcome: highly capable single API function call LLM. They say you could do it with as little as 100 training inputs if you really wanted.

I think this is interesting, but not world-shattering. I could imagine building a nice little service company on it, basically just "send us a git repo and you'll get a helpful function call API for this version of your code which you can hook up to an API endpoint / chatbot".

Limitations are going to be largely around Gemma-2B's skills -- A 2B model isn't super sophisticated. And you can see they specify "<30 tokens" for the prompt. But, I imagine this could be trained quickly enough that it could be part of a release CI process. There are a number of libraries I use that I would like to have access to such a model.

I'd be interested in something that has general knowledge of a large set of packages for a language, and could pull in / finetune / MoE little models for specific repositories I'm coding on. Right now I would rely on either a very large model and hope its knowledge cutoff is right (Claude/GPT-4), or using a lot of a large context window. There might be some Goldilocks version in the middle here which would be helpful in a larger codebase but be faster and more accurate than the cloud monopoly providers.

 

IBM Granite Model

IBM introduced a new set of models called Granite, which are specific for Code generation and completion usage.

Comparison of Granite-8B-Code (Base/Instruct) with other open source (code) LLMs of similar size on HumanEvalPack

 

Coding agents for Enterprise AI are not that many;

  • Meta Llama family
  • Google Gemma
  • DBRX (Databricks),
  • Arctic (Snowflake),
  • Grok,
  • Mixtral 8x22B (MistralAI),
  • Command R+ (Cohere)

What really keeps my eye is, for example, models that have been trained on ABAP programming language, since most of my questions will require an ABAP FM call. Granite indicates it was trained on ABAP. Bravo!

 IBM Granite trained on ABAP?

Granite was trained on ABAP, this is good, lets see how;

 

The problem is none of these datasets contain ABAP Language other than one or two Functions.

In many enterprise contexts, code LLM adoption can be further complicated by factors beyond the performance of the models. For instance, even open models are sometimes plagued by a lack of transparency about the data sources and data processing methods that went into model, which can erode trust in models in mission critical and regulated contexts. Furthermore, license terms in today’s open LLMs can encumber and complicate an enterprise’s ability to use a model.

But, this is not Code Generation, this is about Function Calling

Calling Functions and Tools, we must check the Berkeley Function-Calling Leaderboard (BFCL), to evaluate LLM’s ability to call functions and tools. BFCL is a function-calling dataset with 1700 functions across 4 categories: simple, multiple, parallel, and parallel multiple function calls -

Applications and Services should by-default expect their APIs to be chained with each other when used by agents. To support such a scenario, there needs to be a way to express which APIs can be commutative, associative or distributive with a given set of APIs.

 

 

What we might want to have is a model trained to decide which APIs to call, when to call them, what arguments to pass, and how to best incorporate the results into future token prediction

How to achieve this?

This is done in a self-supervised way, requiring nothing more than a handful of demonstrations for each API. We incorporate a range of tools, including a calculator, a Q&A system, a search engine, a translation system, and a calendar

In order for LMs to use this basic paradigm of using tools, current works mainly leverage inference-time prompting and training-time learning methods.

Inference-time prompting leverages the ability of LMs to learn in-context while Learning by training make LM learn from examples that use these tools during training. The use of tools should be learned in a self-supervised way without requiring large amounts of human annotations

For complex queries that require multiple tools to solve, the common approach is to break down the task and tackle each step sequentially by selecting and using tools with intermediate contexts. 

Tools are mainly aggregated from existing datasets or public APIs, but these benchmarks are limited in domains. Several works scrape more APIs from online sources such as Public APIs, RESTful APIs or the OpenAI plugin list. Nonetheless, as tools are collected from heterogeneous sources, it is challenging to select the best benchmark or unify all these varied benchmarks. 

LLMs can be trained to use tools by combining traditional fine-tuning methods with in-context learning. One approach called ToolkenGPT represents each tool as a token ("toolken") and learns an embedding for it, allowing the LLM to generate tool calls like regular word tokens

Training LLMs to use tools requires careful prompt engineering, key methods for training LLMs on tool use include learning tool embeddings, parameter-efficient fine-tuning, in-context learning from examples, and prompt engineering. Newer approaches aim to combine the strengths of these techniques for flexible and efficient tool integration.

 

If our intention is to build an API database or dataset, we must do it in four stages, is described in four stages: data pre-processing, API database creation, instructions generation, and data validation.

 

1. Data Pre-processing stage involves filtering out files or malfunctioning endpoints, information about the API and its endpoints are extracted.

2. Create API DBs, an API database is created. This database contains instances in JSON format, each holding all relevant information about an endpoint, and the API it belongs to. API call examples should be included in each instance.

3. Instructions Generation by creating high-quality instruction examples and generating instruction candidates. The method involves selecting endpoints, bootstrapping information into templates, and refining the instructions, at this stage, the selected LLM is used to generate instructions based on the examples.

 

4. Data Validation stage involves verifying if the API calls are valid HTTP request examples, checking if the instructions are of high-quality, and selecting the instruction with the best quality for training. The LLM is used for various tasks in this stage, such as labeling instructions as good or bad, and calculating the likelihood of an LLM to recreate the input text used to generate each instruction.

Its clear that LLMs like GPT-4 require significant computational resources, making them inefficient and , for coding pourposes, there is a need for smaller, task-oriented LLMs that maintain functionality while reducing costs, however, smaller models have a higher risk of errors or "hallucinations" and issues with precise output formatting, which is critical for robust software applications, so the solution is headed for LLM Training and Inference, using a dataset of thousands of widely-used APIs from pourpose built datasets, covering diverse functionalities of the application we need to, with the goal  to improve LLMs' ability to select appropriate API functions. This might be done with small models like Codellama7b, Google's Gemma 7B & 2B, and Stable Code 3B.

The technical limitation lies on the 3rd step, if I try to create an OpenAPI spec for a single SAP API, I take for instance the Material Serial Number, service name: API_MATERIALSERIALNUMBER, to create, read and update serial numbers for a material, this API, in its most basic format for OpenAPI spec, will require the actual schemas for the respective entities and actions, in other words, we need to adjust it further than this for a single SAP API;

 

 

 

 

 

 

openapi: 3.0.0
info:
  version: '1.0.0'
  title: 'Material Serial Number API'
  description: 'API to create, read, update, and manage material serial numbers'
servers:
  - url: 'https://api.sap.com/api/OP_API_MATERIALSERIALNUMBER_0001/resource'
paths:
  /MaterialSerialNumber:
    get:
      summary: 'Get entities from MaterialSerialNumber'
      description: 'Read data for all material serial numbers.'
      parameters:
        - in: query
          name: Material
          schema:
            type: string
          description: 'Indicates the unique number that identifies a material.'
          required: true
        - in: query
          name: SerialNumber
          schema:
            type: string
          description: 'Indicates the number of the serialized equipment.'
          required: true
      responses:
        '200':
          description: 'OK'
          content:
            application/json:
              schema:
                # Add schema for MaterialSerialNumber entity
                type: object
    post:
      summary: 'Add new entity to MaterialSerialNumber'
      description: 'Create a material serial number.'
      requestBody:
        content:
          application/json:
            schema:
              # Add schema for MaterialSerialNumber entity
              type: object
      responses:
        '201':
          description: 'Created'
          content:
            application/json:
              schema:
                # Add schema for MaterialSerialNumber entity
                type: object
  /MaterialSerialNumber/SAP__self.CreateMassMaterialSerialNumber:
    post:
      summary: 'Invoke action CreateMassMaterialSerialNumber'
      description: 'Create Mass Material Serial Numbers'
      requestBody:
        content:
          application/json:
            schema:
              # Add schema for CreateMassMaterialSerialNumber action
              type: object
      responses:
        '200':
          description: 'OK'
          content:
            application/json:
              schema:
                # Add schema for MaterialSerialNumber entity
                type: object
  /MaterialSerialNumber/{Material}/{SerialNumber}:
    get:
      summary: 'Get entity from MaterialSerialNumber by key'
      description: 'Read data for a particular material serial number.'
      parameters:
        - in: path
          name: Material
          schema:
            type: string
          required: true
        - in: path
          name: SerialNumber
          schema:
            type: string
          required: true
      responses:
        '200':
          description: 'OK'
          content:
            application/json:
              schema:
                # Add schema for MaterialSerialNumber entity
                type: object
    patch:
      summary: 'Update entity in MaterialSerialNumber'
      description: 'Update StockInformation for an existing material serial number using PATCH request.'
      parameters:
        - in: path
          name: Material
          schema:
            type: string
          required: true
        - in: path
          name: SerialNumber
          schema:
            type: string
          required: true
      requestBody:
        content:
          application/json:
            schema:
              # Add schema for MaterialSerialNumber entity
              type: object
      responses:
        '200':
          description: 'OK'
          content:
            application/json:
              schema:
                # Add schema for MaterialSerialNumber entity
                type: object
  /MaterialSerialNumber/{Material}/{SerialNumber}/SAP__self.ChangeMaintenancePlant:
    post:
      summary: 'Invoke action ChangeMaintenancePlant'
      requestBody:
        content:
          application/json:
            schema:
              # Add schema for ChangeMaintenancePlant action
              type: object
      responses:
        '204':
          description: 'No Content'
  # Add other paths and operations similarly
  /MaterialSerialNumber/{Material}/{SerialNumber}/_Partner:
    get:
      summary: 'Get entities from related _Partner'
      responses:
        '200':
          description: 'OK'
          content:
            application/json:
              schema:
                # Add schema for _Partner entity
                type: object
    post:
      summary: 'Add new entity to related _Partner'
      requestBody:
        content:
          application/json:
            schema:
              # Add schema for _Partner entity
              type: object
      responses:
        '201':
          description: 'Created'
          content:
            application/json:
              schema:
                # Add schema for _Partner entity
                type: object
  /MaterialSerialNumberPartner/{Material}/{SerialNumber}/{Equipment}/{PartnerFunction}/{EquipmentPartnerObjectNmbr}/_EquipmentMaterialSerialNumber:
    get:
      summary: 'Get related _EquipmentMaterialSerialNumber'
      responses:
        '200':
          description: 'OK'
          content:
            application/json:
              schema:
                # Add schema for _EquipmentMaterialSerialNumber entity
                type: object
  # Add other paths and operations similarly
  /$batch:
    post:
      summary: 'Send a group of requests'
      requestBody:
        content:
          application/json:
            schema:
              # Add schema for batch request
              type: object
      responses:
        '200':
          description: 'OK'
          content:
            application/json:
              schema:
                # Add schema for batch response
                type: object

 

 

Conclusion

I describe what Function Calling is and what capability brings in the models, and which models have been tested with Function Calling mechanisms. I have been trying to understand if any of the existing models have been trained with SAP Function Calling capabilities, and what it would require to fine tune a small model with SAP APIs, just in case you want to explore it, what it would take.

We discussed several options, Multi Modal, like Octopus, Tool Augmented LLMs, which is the most explored option and has been around for a while, Model Chaining and API Agents which is a very recent methodology as well as Tool Usage Planning which requires further investigation, all of them will require worth a lot of knowledge from the SAP team to make it work correctly, I also particularly started creating an SAP API Dataset and made it public on Hugging Face, which I will describe in a future blog.

Its almost June 2024 and we are entering what Gartner calls the "Trough of Disillusionment", although this methodology is not enough for SAP, keep the faith, don't loose trust on Generative AI, we'll get there.

entry.title: Get Ready for Sapphire 2024 with Three New SAP Business Technology Platform Use Cases
entry.content:
Can you believe it? It’s already time for SAP Sapphire 2024, happening in Orlando and Barcelona, as well as virtually. Join us for days of inspiration, innovation, and insight into the latest trends and technologies at SAP shaping the future of business.

 

SAP Business Technology Platform at SAPPHIRE

In this blog, I'll explore new use cases for 2024, focusing on SAP's key themes. We'll see how SAP Business Technology Platform, powered by Generative AI, can transform the value derived from other SAP business applications. These use cases come with their respective Discovery Center Missions and Business Accelerators, making your journey with SAP BTP both easy and efficient.

If you're heading to the in-person event in Orlando, make sure to catch our session, "Explore Business Scenarios Enabled by SAP BTP That You Can Implement Today." My colleague Denise Ma and I will be sharing practical tips and insights to help you jump-start your SAP BTP journey. It's a must-add to your agenda! Don't forget to stop by the SAP BTP: For Business Applications demo station, where our team will be ready to answer all your questions and help you get the most out of SAP BTP. Also, check out the following sessions by the team:

    • BTP108 | Transforming the future of Fresenius: RISE with SAP and SAP Analytics Cloud
    • BTP110 | Using cross-departmental data for analysis and planning with SAP S/4HANA
    • BTP111 | Driving the data and analytics strategy with SAP software at Lundin Mining
    • BTP154 | Enhance your investment in SAP S/4HANA Cloud with SAP BTP
    • BTP190 | How Nestle and Nokia accelerated transformation of SAP S/4HANA with SAP BTP

 

New Business Technology Platform Use Cases

In today's fast-moving business world, keeping up isn't enough; you need to be innovative, agile, and efficient. That's where SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP) comes in. It helps you extend and personalize SAP applications, seamlessly integrate different systems, and empower your team with AI to streamline processes, improve experiences, and make confident decisions. Let's check out three exciting new SAP BTP use cases for 2024:

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Use Case #1. Improve operations and decision-making with the power of Generative AI 

You: You're at a critical crossroads in deciding whether to prioritize modernizing your current landscape of business and processes or pivot towards AI-driven innovations. As per McKinsey’s 2023 survey*, Generative AI is already being regularly used at work among 22 percent of the survey respondents, and Generative AI is expected to significantly disrupt industry competition within three years. However, the risks and complexities involved in leveraging AI based on your business data are preventing you from making the jump. These include the challenges of integrating AI with third-party providers and existing systems, especially when the data involved is sensitive, raising concerns about accuracy and reliability.

How SAP BTP can help: Now is the ideal time to embrace AI, thoughtfully navigate these challenges, and realize the transformative potential it holds for your business. SAP BTP infuses business-ready, trusted, and user-friendly AI capabilities across the platform, to boost your business’ productivity and build AI solutions with your business-relevant and reliable data. Using AI in BTP can also boost the ROI of your existing SAP BTP investments, together with SAP Business applications and other third-party applications. Additionally, SAP BTP improves security by providing increased visibility and control over your AI usage with easily managed access to frontier LLMs, so your data is handled responsibly. Read more about SAP Business AI here.

Check out these sessions:

Orlando
  • BTP189 | Data in the Age of AI 
  • BTP192 | AI in SAP BTP – Vision and strategy
  • BTP197 | Take a lap through AI in SAP BTP
Barcelona
  • BTP210 | Data in the age of AI: Strategy and innovations
  • BTP218 | Unlocking AI's potential: The crucial role of data
  • BTP229 | ABAP Cloud model and generative AI: A power couple to keep your core clean
Virtual
  • PAR309v | Unlock the potential of your data assets: Data-driven decision-making
  • PAR310v | Transforming business with AI – Unlock value and performance

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Use Case #2. Empower employees with enterprise-wide self-service portals

You: Imagine your work monitor crowded with a patchwork of open applications, each window representing a different task you need to manage. Throughout the day, you find yourself clicking back and forth incessantly, switching between these various programs and interfaces. Your business relies on a mix of SAP and third-party solutions, each with its own unique user interface and experience. Harvard Business Review’s study in 2022** provides an example of a Fortune 500 consumer goods organization, where each person in a single supply-chain interaction has to switch about 350 times between 22 different applications and unique websites. Employees need to be trained on each of these solutions, which makes employee onboarding difficult. Many of them must log in to multiple systems to do their day-to-day work. The myriad of entry points to data across different lines of business only adds complexity to the mix.

How SAP BTP can help: SAP BTP answers the call with SAP Build Work Zone, advanced edition. By consolidating tools and offering an intuitive and unified entry point for SAP and 3rd party applications, business processes, and information, SAP Build Work Zone fosters collaboration and efficiency. Role-based access paves the way for streamlined workflows, heightened productivity, and a culture of innovation. Giving employees a simplified user experience and access to multiple systems and processes, enabling them to build interactive workspaces visually to better engage with their teams and external stakeholders, truly unifies employee experience and increases productivity. Read more about SAP Build Work Zone here.

 

Orlando
  • BTP120 | Lockheed Martin: Fostering one employee experience with SAP Build Work Zone
  • BTP131 | What's new with SAP Build Work Zone and the SAP Build Apps tool
  • BTP186 | Transformation at Hatch Ltd. with SAP S/4HANA and SAP Build Work Zone
Barcelona
  • PAR240 | CEPSA ‘HR’evolution: Empowering people, transforming human resources
Virtual
  • HCM108v | Close the skills gap with total workforce management

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Use Case #3. Supercharge developer productivity with SAP Build Code and generative AI

You: You are an SAP developer, and many hours of your day are spent navigating complex application logic and checking data models. After all, IDC’s research*** states that developers spend 60% of their time on operational responsibilities, such as the implementation of automation, code testing and execution, while only 15% of their time is spent on coding. All those development and testing experiences while striving to shorten lead times to market pose significant hurdles for professional developers. Speed and flexibility while securing productivity are difficult to achieve.

How SAP BTP can help: SAP Build Code provides generative AI-based code development with Joule copilot, empowering developers to overcome these challenges. It offers an intuitive, turn-key design time and runtime environment for coding, testing, integrations, and application lifecycle management. Use AI to generate app logic, data models, and test scripts, and experience how SAP Build Code accelerates time-to-value for development projects and supercharges developer productivity. Read more about SAP Build Code here.

 

Orlando
  • BTP117 | Generative AI–based code development from SAP Build Code
  • BTP118 | Discover generative AI-based code development from SAP Build Code
  • BTP119 | Application development with SAP Build and SAP Build Code: Road map review
Barcelona
  • BTP138 | Generative AI–based code development from SAP Build Code
Virtual
  • BTP224v | Extend SAP S/4HANA with SAP Build and SAP Build Code

 

Honourable Mention:

Accelerate Financial Planning & Analysis

You: Working and collaborating across the organization gets overwhelming, as Financial Planning & Analysis is not a one-person or even one-department job; the CEO, the offices of CFO, and the other stakeholders across the business all hold key business data and processes crucial to FP&A, but it seems like each area has their own rules and data in different places. This causes delays in accessing the real-time data, impeding proactive planning and agile decision-making.

How SAP BTP can help: With the power of data integration, visualization, and predictive analytics, SAP Analytics Cloud for Planning serves as a reporting, analytics, and planning tool in one. It allows business users to access current real-time data in a simple and timely manner, eliminating delays in the planning process. Your financial planning projects can be fast-tracked with SAP’s prebuilt business accelerators, and activating predictive analytics with Machine Learning capabilities is as easy as a single click. Read more on SAP’s Financial Planning and Analysis solutions here.

 

To wrap up

SAP Business Technology Platform provides organizations with the tools and capabilities to thrive in the digital age. Through the lens of Generative AI, self-service portals, and developer productivity tools, SAP BTP not only addresses challenges but also unlocks opportunities for growth and innovation. It's not just about staying ahead; it's about leading the charge towards excellence. Now that these three new use cases have piqued your interest, visit SAP Business Technology Platform In Action community page for the full list of SAP BTP Use Cases and other resources. 

With a lineup of industry experts, hands-on workshops, and networking opportunities, Sapphire 2024 is not to be missed. We look forward to seeing you in Orlando, Barcelona, or virtually and sharing more about what SAP Business Technology Platform can do for you and your organization. Get ready to unlock your business's full potential and explore the possibilities of the intelligent enterprise.

 

Additional Resources

  • HCM 102 | Technology strategy and road map for SAP SuccessFactors solutions session to learn more about how SAP BTP works with SAP SuccessFactors.

 

*The state of AI in 2023: Generative AI’s breakout year. (2023, August 1). McKinsey & Company. https://www.mckinsey.com/capabilities/quantumblack/our-insights/the-state-of-ai-in-2023-generative-ais-breakout-year

**Murty, R. N. (2022, November 1). How much time and energy do we waste toggling between applications? Harvard Business Review. https://hbr.org/2022/08/how-much-time-and-energy-do-we-waste-toggling-between-applications

***IDC: Generative AI and turnkey development platforms can improve developer productivity. (n.d.). SAP. https://www.sap.com/canada/documents/2024/01/fe86d503-a57e-0010-bca6-c68f7e60039b.html

entry.title: Asset Depreciation value changed in the middle of the useful life
entry.content:
Dear Expert,

Good Day.

Our business is facing an issue with an asset.

One Asset has a useful life of 5 years. The depreciation key uses using straight-line method. The asset started capitalizing in 2019 and depreciated since 2019. From 2019 to 2022, the depreciation value was posted 8,77,000 per annum but in 2023, the depreciation value was posted 7,65,000. I checked all the change documents but nothing was changed during the useful life.

Could you please let me know what is the reason for changing of depreciation value in the 5th year?

 

Regards,

Shreekumar

entry.title: OOABAP

You have a superclass super1 and a subclass sub1 of super1. Each class has an instance constructor and a static constructor. The first statement of the program created an instance of sub1. In which sequence will the constructors be executed?

a. Class constructor of super1

b. Instance constructor of sub1

c. Instance constructor of super 1

d. Class constructor of sub1.

entry.title: Re: New in Production Accounting – Event-Based Production Cost Posting
entry.content: Thank you Eva for sharing the blog on Product Cost by Period. It helps a lot in understanding the concept. As part of assembly backflush, gr, gi and activity confirmation are posted. Still, I am not able to get variance. There is no WIP as Reporting Point confirmation is not active. Could you please let me know what might be causing the issue ? I believe there is no separate calculation and posting of variance required when event based (3FO) functionality is active.

entry.title: Here is Your Top Ten Sessions Agenda at SAPPHIRE Orlando for Supply Chain Planners!
entry.content:
Do you want to reduce supply chain risk and make your business more resilient and agile? Then join SAPPHIRE and experience an agenda full of talks, demos, and success stories and learn how AI-enabled, synchronized planning can break down silos, create flexible plans, and identify supply risks. Discover the benefits of probabilistic planning and embedded Business AI in SAP Integrated Business Planning for Supply Chain. Hear how industry leaders like Bailey Metal, Entegris, NVIDIA and Mohawk boosted their supply chain planning with SAP IBP. Don’t miss this opportunity to experience simulations and compare scenarios for fast, informed decision-making through predictive, automated, and AI-enabled digital business solutions at our demo station. Together, we can help you achieve a risk-resilient and AI-enabled supply chain, so you can navigate uncertainty and accelerate sustainable growth. Examine the below list of Top Ten sessions for Planners starting on Tuesday morning.

SAPPHIRE Agenda Link for Planners

 

1. Reduce supply chain risk with AI-enabled, synchronized planning - Strategy Talk

  • Tuesday, June 04, 2024
  • 11:00 a.m. - 11:20 a.m.
  • Theater 6

Explore how synchronized planning breaks down silos across planning and execution, and creates flexible, agile, and AI-enabled plans. You can use AI to better forecast demand and identify supply risks and the probability of disruptions while running simulations and comparing scenarios for fast, AI-enabled decision-making.

 

2. Probabilistic planning and generative AI - Solution Demo

  • Tuesday, June 04, 2024
  • 11:30 a.m. - 11:50 a.m.
  • Theater 132

Incorporate probabilistic planning and generative AI in your supply chain planning. We look at current capabilities in the SAP Integrated Business Planning for Supply Chain solution, planned developments, and how these hot topics relate to the Joule copilot and SAP Business AI technology.

 

3. Expand your business with a risk-resilient and AI-enabled supply chain - Strategy Talk

  • Tuesday, June 04, 2024
  • 01:30 p.m. - 01:50 p.m.
  • Theater 15

Grow and protect your business with a data-driven, digital supply chain to connect your enterprise with the outside world. This enables you to connect supply chain processes with your business imperatives, contextualizing decisions by linking operational and business data together and collaborating with your trading partners digitally.

 

4. How supply chain leaders are addressing risk with better planning - Customer Panel Discussion

  • Tuesday, June 04, 2024
  • 03:30 p.m. - 04:10 p.m.
  • Theater 5

Industry leaders mitigate supply chain risks by improving supply chain transparency and proactively running simulations and what-if scenarios for informed decision-making. Learn how Nvidia Corporation, semiconductor supplier Entegris Inc. and Baily Metal Products enhanced their operations using the SAP Integrated Business Planning for Supply Chain.

 

5. SAP and Google Cloud technology accelerates AI-driven supply chains - Strategy Talk

  • Wednesday, June 05, 2024
  • 11:00 a.m. - 11:20 a.m.
  • Theater 16

New generative AI technology from SAP and Google Cloud is revolutionizing supply chains. Gemini models from Google Cloud Vertex AI and Cortex Framework integrated with the SAP Integrated Business Planning for Supply Chain solution help you navigate uncertainty, identify events, and drive competitive edge in the value chain.

 

6. Boosting Mohawk’s supply chain planning with SAP IBP - Customer Success Story

  • Wednesday, June 05, 2024
  • 01:00 p.m. - 01:20 p.m.
  • Theater 5

Learn how the SAP Integrated Business Planning for Supply Chain solution (SAP IBP) helped Mohawk improve advanced planning capabilities across demand management, inventory optimization, and supply planning. We look at the flooring company’s journey to establishing a financially integrated, highly responsive supply chain.

 

7. Achieve supply chain resiliency through planning excellence - Strategy Talk

  • Wednesday, June 05, 2024
  • 01:30 p.m. - 01:50 p.m.
  • Theater 6

See how you can eliminate planning risks throughout your supply network, manage disruptions and variability, and accelerate sustainable growth. We explore how you can automate processes, run simulations, and align decisions across departments with the new RISE with SAP advanced supply chain planning package.

 

8. Vision and road map for a risk-resilient supply chain with SAP IBP - Road Map Review

  • Wednesday, June 05, 2024
  • 02:00 p.m. - 02:40 p.m.
  • S320 G-H - Road Map Room 7

Learn how the SAP Integrated Business Planning for Supply Chain (SAP IBP) solution supports intelligent supply chain planning and drives risk resiliency with innovations in key areas that enable automated planning decisions. We explore what benefits AI and characteristic-based planning approaches bring to disrupted and regulated supply chains.

 

9. Empowering cloud supply chains with Microsoft and SAP - Deep Dive

  • Wednesday, June 05, 2024
  • 02:30 p.m. - 03:10 p.m.
  • Theater 5

In just three years, Microsoft Corporation integrated SAP software and distributed ledger technology for cloud supply chains to enable real-time collaboration and traceability. We explore an approach that helps digitalize assets, harnesses AI, and supports billions of transactions, as well as looking at future plans.

 

10. Supply chain planning solutions from SAP

Demo Station

  • All Day
  • Show Floor

A risk-resilient, connected, and collaborative supply chain responds quickly and cost-effectively to changes in supply, demand, and market conditions. Experience simulations and compare scenarios for fast, informed decision-making through predictive, automated, and AI-enabled digital business solutions.

entry.title: Customer & Partner Roundtable for SAP BTP ABAP Environment #13
entry.content:
👉 The call for contributions for one of the upcoming roundtables is open! 

 

If you want to show a demo or share a use case scenario for SAP BTP ABAP Environment send us an email and we will get back to you.
 

Introduction

 

As SAP BTP ABAP environment (aka Steampunk) and ABAP Cloud became more and more popular inside and outside of SAP, there is a high demand for rolling out the latest product news and updates, asking questions, and of course showing demos. 

💡 If you weren’t able to join one of our previous roundtables, you can find the slides presented, recordings, and further references in this GitHub repository.


Meeting Information

When:                                                                      
  • June 25th, 10:00 - 11:00 AM CET  – Zoom Meeting (please register in advance) 
 
Who:
  • All interested customers, partners, and stakeholders are invited to join and exchange ideas and feedback with others and the product team
  • Steampunk team: Frank Jentsch (Product Lead for SAP BTP ABAP Environment), Katharina Wurz (Developer ABAP Development Tools), Thomas Fiedler (Area Product Owner ABAP Developer Tools), Iwona Hahn & Burcu Karlidag (BTP ABAP Product Manager)

Preliminary Agenda:
  • Product news
  • Baseline Support for ABAP Test Cockpit in SAP BTP incl. demo

Looking forward to meeting you!
 


Check out our SAP Business Technology ABAP Environment page in SAP Community for product updates and upcoming events.

entry.title: Half/full pallet order quantity prerequisite for sales order creation
entry.content:
Hi, our client wants to have prerequisite at the time of sales order creation where certain customers are allowed to create sales orders with order quantity in multiple of half/full pallets only. This check should be active only for certain customer and not all customers. 

Ex - Product XYZ has base unit of PC and sales unit of PAL where 1 PAL = 24 PC.

If a customer sends in an EDI order for 15 PC, the EDI message should error out and a failure notification should be sent to a business user whereas, if the same customer sends in an EDI order for 48 PC, system should allow sales order creation as 48 is a multiple of 24 (2 x 24 = 48). 

 

Is there a solution available in SAP S4 Public Cloud to fulfil this requirement?

entry.title: Query on memory utilization by SAP_CONVERT_TO_TEX_FORMAT
entry.content:
Hello All,

Facing a weird issue with memory consumption on usage of SAP_CONVERT_TO_TEX_FORMAT. PFB the details of the issue:

Program logic:

We are fetching 200,000 records into an internal table and using FM 'SAP_CONVERT_TO_TEX_FORMAT' to separate entries with '|' as separator, and data is downloaded as zip file from the internal table. 

Issue:

We have to download close to 1,900,000 entries, so we are splitting entries into 200,000 as a single block and processing this data.

During fifth iteration 184,000 entries are copied into internal table (as per period logic), and once the data is downloaded and goes for next iteration of 200,000, it fails with error 'TSV_TNEW_PAGE_ALLOC_FAILED'

Analysis:

While I was during memory analysis in debug mode, i've made a strange observation.

Until the 184,000 entries were downloaded I see the total memory consumption as 3,123,909,208 (ABAP mem was 2,534,342,234, whereas when the new set of 200,000 entries were being downloaded it suddenly increases to 4,514,178,208 (ABAP application total memory was 3,536,909,590)

This was observed after usage of SAP_CONVERT_TO_TEX_FORMAT.

Any pointers in sudden jump of total memory in memory sizes external session, when ABAP application memory usage remains same?

Thanks & Regards,

Naga

entry.title: Re: Can I extend a certain app? With Clean Core? How?
entry.content:
Dear @mlauber,

1. The implication is that if the OData service is based of gateway project, the options are limited. This you rightly pointed out in the article. Thanks for confirming. 

2. I thought the oData service used by a Fiori App is not considered a public interface; Building a custom App with such a service is not considered clean core, even though there is no modification of standard code or any package access violation. If that is the case, I wonder how it could be clean core to copy this interface, which is what you do when you create a new service based on it. During an upgrade the standard app could change the service in many ways. The custom developed service now has to apply these modifications manually since it is a copy and not an extension. I see it having all the risks of a copying a standard object. But, thanks for sharing SAP's perspective on this.

entry.title: Re: SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA - Empowering Your Homepage: Enabling My Home for SAP S/4HANA 2023 FPS0
entry.content:
@MelaiSaldua Thank you for the feedback. Below are answers to your questions.

  1. My Home is a personal page so any there's no "default look or configuration" that us developers we can give for all users, right? 
    You are right, it's all based on personalization. However, this has a fuzzy answer. The default 8 colored pages under the Pages section (without any personalization), are derived from the user-assigned FLP content i.e. first 8 pages, so they can be guessed and designed in advance. The same goes for UI Insights to an extent. Of course, the end user can then personalize it.
    Additionally, You could also create My Home App variants using "Adapt UI" (Key User Extensibility) and assign them to users. Here you can control the visibility and order of these sections as a whole and also the news feed.
  2. Another question is if our users are not using the standard My Inbox but instead using a z copy of My Inbox, it is possible to add it their Tasks section? 
    Yes, as discussed above My Home uses standard OData service /IWPGW/TASKPROCESSING to fetch To Dos. However, if you are interested in amending the standard, you can use the SAP-delivered UI5 component as a reference (PRODUCT_HOMES1) and tweak it as per your requirement. The component can controlled via SPACES_CUSTOM_HOME_COMPONENT_ID (Launchpad Configuration Parameter) as explained above.

entry.title: UUIDs and Unique Semantic Primary Keys
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

I'm working with RAP in BTP and have a question regarding UUIDs and semantic primary keys.

What is the best and recommended way to guarantee the uniqueness of a semantic primary key?

In RAP and OData V4, UUIDs provide us with the ability to create entities in draft mode without needing to validate the keys in the creation popup, which has limitations for more complex checking and responding with messages. Often, these validations result in the popup closing, causing the user to lose the data they have entered and forcing them to re-enter it.

I believe using automatically generated UUIDs allows us to move directly to the edit screen, where we have more control for validations and displaying messages without losing the input data.

I have two options already envisioned for helping me with this issue:

  • Using a validation for create and updating the BO (select in the table and check for uniqueness). Which I'm already using but I think there might be a better way for it
  • Using an unique index in the table and handling the errors. I've not implemented this solution but I imagine would be less cumbersome to implement.

I'm looking forward to hear the input from the colleagues,

Jayme

entry.title: Synchronous Goods movements failing/missing for extended production integration
entry.content:
Hello,

 

Synchronous Goods movements failing/missing for extended production integration.

 

We want to use the extended production integration with PMR. Our System is a S/4 HANA 2022 System with FPS 02.
 
We want to consume order specific staged parts in the psa with direct backflush which should be possible since release 1909 with the help of synchronous goods movements.
 

 

 
sap help: https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_ON-PREMISE/9832125c23154a179bfa1784cdc9577a/ae9beb535cbb5d1ee10000000a441470.html?locale=en-US&version=2022.002
 
"If you are using direct goods movements, you can enter a backflush in Production and the system processes the confirmation without a consumption posting delivery. Backflush-related consumption using direct goods movements works for components staged using single-order staging or cross-order staging."
 
what we did:
 
We adjusted the Production Scheduling Profile  in a way that synchronous movements are allowed:
 

 

(setting 2 = only GI should also be fine. also tested)
 
 
furtermore in general the setting is active in regards to erp interface
 

 

 
example  (among many others) 
  • After releasing the order,
  • triggering staging/creating a pmr,
  • creating staging tasks with RF/webdynrpo app staging
  • for production for order specific parts
  • (incl. Badi implemented for correct qty) and
  • confirming we want to use co11n for cornfirmation 
  • with the result that the staged stock (with pmr reservation) is NOT consumed and 
  • we are getting cogi entries

  • (!!) important: we recognized via slg1: among some ppf errors, system is somehow trying to create a delivery in the background with qty zero which seems incorrect since we want to post sychronous/direct

    br

entry.title: Re: MDK Web + mobile app
entry.content:
The InitializeOffline.action defined in your MDK project is of type Action.Type.OfflineOData.Initialize which is applicable to mobile only. This action type won't work (and is not supported) on MDK web, which is why your MDK web doesn't initialize the service.

As we expanded MDK support for web some time ago, we introduced a new action type Action.Type.ODataService.Initialize that will work for both mobile (online/offline) and web (online only) platforms in a given MK project. This way, you can run the same MDK metadata on both mobile and web platforms(keeping web runtime limitations in mind). 

You can:

  • either update the _Type in your existing InitializeOffline.action (Change OfflineOData to ODataService).
  • OR use a platform function to call different actions on different platforms using $PLT(ios, android, web). 

For example:

 

 

"OnLaunch": ["$(PLT,/MDKApp/Actions/Service/InitializeOffline.action,/MDKApp/Actions/Service/InitializeOffline.action,/MDKApp/Actions/Service/InitializeOnlineWeb.action)"],

 

 

Hope this helps.

entry.title: Attachment Handling In SAP CPI| Send attachment to XI receiver
entry.content:
Hello everyone,

In this short blog, we will discuss attachment handling.

Our goal is to pick a random file, create an XML main payload from the file's properties, and send it to the receiver with the file content as an attachment.

 

The iflow looks like below.

In above flow, we will pick a random file from the FTP source directory and use LIP to implement the attachment logic.

 

Content modifier 

in this step, we will set Content-Type and FileTimestamp.

 

Groovy Script Set File content as Attachment

 

We will now use the standard script provided by SAP for attachment handling.

Note for your reference --> https://help.sap.com/docs/cloud-integration/sap-cloud-integration/create-attachments

 

 

import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message
import org.apache.camel.impl.DefaultAttachment
import javax.mail.util.ByteArrayDataSource

def Message processData(Message message) {

    // Get content type
    def headers = message.getHeaders()
    def contentType = headers.get("Content-Type")
    
    // Define suffix
    def suffix = contentType.substring(contentType.indexOf("/")+1)
    if (suffix == "plain") {
        suffix = 'txt'
    }

    // Get the attachment name from the header
    def attachmentName = headers.get("CamelFileName")
    if (!attachmentName) {
        // Default to 'attachment' if the header is not provided
        attachmentName = 'attachment.' + suffix
    } else {
        // Ensure the attachment name has the correct suffix
        if (!attachmentName.endsWith('.' + suffix)) {
            attachmentName += '.' + suffix
        }
    }

    // Get message payload as input stream
    def body = message.getBody(InputStream.class)

    // Set MIME type
    def dataSource = new ByteArrayDataSource(body, contentType)

    // Construct a DefaultAttachment object
    def attachment = new DefaultAttachment(dataSource)

    // Add the attachment to the message
    message.addAttachmentObject(attachmentName, attachment)

    return message
}

 

You can adjust this script according to your requirements.

Groovy Script Set file Properties as payload

Now, we will use another Groovy script to create the payload as shown below:

 

<ns:Attachment xmlns:ns='namespace'>
<FileName> Filename </FileName>
<Directory> Directory </Directory>
<FileType>  Content_Type </FileType>
<FileSize> Content_Length </FileSize>
<FileTimestamp> TimeStamp </FileTimestamp>
</ns:Attachment>

 

 

 

To create these files, we are going to use the headers below:

CamelFileName   --> processed file name

CamelFileParent  --> Directory Name

Content-Type       --> Content-Type

CamelFileLength  --> File Size

FileTimestamp     --> FileTimestamp

Note->  You may need to add some header in runtime configuration --> allowed  header.

Here is the complete script to create the payload in the required format:

 

import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message
import groovy.xml.MarkupBuilder

def Message processData(Message message) {
    // Retrieve headers from the message
    def fileName = message.getHeader("CamelFileName", String.class)
    def directory = message.getHeader("CamelFileParent", String.class)
    def fileType = message.getHeader("Content-Type",String.class)
    def fileSize = message.getHeader("CamelFileLength", String.class)
    def fileTimestamp = message.getHeader("FileTimestamp", String.class)
    
    // Log the retrieved headers for debugging purposes
    def messageLog = messageLogFactory.getMessageLog(message)
    if (messageLog != null) {
        messageLog.addAttachmentAsString("Debug - FileName", fileName ?: "null", "text/plain")
        messageLog.addAttachmentAsString("Debug - Directory", directory ?: "null", "text/plain")
        messageLog.addAttachmentAsString("Debug - FileType", fileType ?: "null", "text/plain")
        messageLog.addAttachmentAsString("Debug - FileSize", fileSize?.toString() ?: "null", "text/plain")
        messageLog.addAttachmentAsString("Debug - FileTimestamp", fileTimestamp ?: "null", "text/plain")
    }
    
    // Create XML structure
    def writer = new StringWriter()
    def xml = new MarkupBuilder(writer)
    xml.'ns:Attachment'('xmlns:ns': 'namespace') {
        'FileName'(fileName ?: "")
        'Directory'(directory ?: "")
        'FileType'(fileType ?: "")
        'FileSize'(fileSize ?: "")
        'FileTimestamp'(fileTimestamp ?: "")
    }
    
    // Set the XML structure as the message payload
    message.setBody(writer.toString())
    
    return message
}

 


 Now, configure the Send and XI adapters, and deploy the integration flow. You will see the final output as shown below:

Here under Inbound Payloads you can see the main document and the attachment document containing the processed file content.

conclusion

 we've set up an integration flow that picks a random file from the FTP source directory, processes it using standard SAP scripts for attachment handling, and creates the main payload with specified headers. After configuring the Send and XI adapters and deploying the flow, the final output includes both the main document and the attachment document with the processed file content.


Feel free to optimize this solution further as needed, and I welcome any feedback or improvements 🙂.

entry.title: New xP&A content & composites performance
entry.content:
Hi SAP,

We've just installed all the newest SAP xP&A business content on SAP Analytics Cloud, and notice that a lot of optimized design stories & composites are now being used, with advanced scripting & new navigation menu's.

I wanted to raise a question (or concern) about startup performance of those stories, because each story seems to take +35 seconds to load, and I've been trying this after multiple browser refreshes.

When looking into the "Performance Optimization" function (using ctrl+a) and after further analysis it seems to me that:
- loading the story from url into browser -> adds +- 17 seconds
- loading the initialization script (new NavMenu loading only) -> adds +- 8 seconds
- loading the initialization script (other stuff) -> adds +- 5 seconds

When clicking in the navigation menu, this will open a new story each time, so this will be +35 seconds of front-end waiting time for the users each time they move to the next step/action in their process flow.

My question is: Is this the expected 'optimized' user experience?
And is this considered the 'best practice'? Or did I miss something?

entry.title: Re: Enable Now Web Assistant is covering the application...
entry.content:
Hi Anna,

I assume that we are talking about SAP Companion here and not SAP Companion for Desktop running as SAP Companion.  If we are talking about SAP Companion for Desktop, the SAP Companion for Desktop toolbar will cover the right edge of the application because it sits as a separate layer over the application.

If we are talking about SAP Companion, when you open SAP Companion the application screen should slide to the left to leave space for the SAP Companion toolbar but this does not always happen.  I suspect that it is an integration issue in most cases where it does not happen.  You have two options:

1. If it's SAP Companion running in an SAP portal, e.g. S/4HANA, raise a support ticket using the Component ID KM-SEN-CMP and get support to take a look
2. Wait for the upgrade to SAP Companion, which will soon be available as a floating window.  Once SAP Companion is a floating window it can be moved to any part of the screen so will no longer be an issue.

Regards
Shane
